content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 23, 1994

Frankie Rutledge
Town Clerk
5555 Skyway
Paradise, CA  95989

RE:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-94-016

Dear Ms. Rutledge:

     You have asked for advice concerning your duties as a filing
officer under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

     What is your obligation as a filing officer when information
in a newspaper indicates that a group of citizens has made
expenditures in connection with an initiative proposal to
disincorporate a town?

CONCLUSION

     As a filing officer, when you receive information through
newspaper articles that citizens are making expenditures in
connection with an effort to qualify a measure for the ballot, you
have no duty to notify the individuals of possible filing
obligations. However, in an effort to assist persons who may be
unaware of the disclosure requirements of the Act, we encourage
filing officers to contact such persons and notify them of the
requirements.

     On the other hand, once you possess a record, such as a
statement of Organization indicating that a committee exists, you
have a duty to notify the committee of its filing obligation if
the committee fails to report.

_________________________
1/     Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

A-94-016
Page 2


     With regard to disincorporation matters, a proponent's
obligation to report expenditures does not begin until the issue
becomes a "measure." A disincorporation proposal does not become a
"measure" until the Board of Supervisors places the proposal on
the ballot.
                                FACTS

     You are the town clerk of Paradise. It has come to your
attention that a group of individuals has placed a newspaper
advertisement announcing a petition effort underway to
disincorporate Paradise. You have not received any campaign
disclosure statements from the proponents of the disincorporation
proposal.

                                ANALYSIS

     Filing officers are required to notify promptly all persons
and known committees who have failed to file statements. (Section
81010.) In addition, Regulation 18110(a)(1) and (2) provide:

     (a) It shall be the duty of a filing officer pursuant
to Section 81010(b) in connection with the filing
of candidate and committee campaign statements to
determine whether required documents have been
filed.

          (1) In order to fulfill his or her duty to
determine whether required documents have been
filed, if the filing officer is aware that a
candidate or committee has an obligation to file a
campaign statement and has failed to do so, the
filing officer shall notify the candidate or
committee of the obligation to file a statement.

          (2) In determining whether required documents have
been filed, the filing officer shall not be required
to conduct any investigation to determine whether or
not a committee or candidate has an obligation to
file a campaign statement. This subsection is not
intended to prevent the filing officer from
performing such an investigation.

     A filing officer's duty to notify a person of an obligation
to file a statement begins when the filing officer receives an
official document establishing the committee's filing obligation,
such as a Statement of Organization (Form 410). A filing officer
is not under an obligation to seek out or conduct an investigation
to determine whether a citizens group seeking a filing requirement
because of an article in a newspaper. (Jorgenson Advice Letter,
No. A-92-406.) However, whenever it appears that a person may
have a filing requirement, filing officers are encouraged to
contact and notify them of the campaign disclosure requirements of
the Act.

A-94-016
Page 3


     The Act provides that campaign disclosure obligations will be
incurred by persons who make payments for political purposes.
(Sections 82013 and 82025.) A payment is made for political
purposes if it is intended to influence the actions of the voters
for or against the qualification or passage of any measure.
(Regulation 18225.)

     The Commission has held that a proponent's obligation to
report expenditures in support of a ballot proposal begins when
the proposal becomes a "measure." (In re Fontana (1976) 2 FPPC
Ops. 25.) With regard to incorporation (or disincorporation)
matters, that does not occur until the Board of Supervisors places
the proposal on the ballot. (Id. at 28.) Therefore, the citizens
group formed to disincorporate the city of Paradise is not
required to file a campaign statement (Form 419) with your office
until the issue becomes a measure. (Martini Advice Letter, No. A-
93-378.)

     If you have any questions regarding this letter, please
contact me at 916/322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steve Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        By: Lynda Cassady
                                            Assistant Division Chief
